parentQuestion: A newly developed antibiotic specifically targets bacterial ribosomes without affecting human ribosomes. Why is this selectivity possible?

options: The cellular structure that makes proteins
The control center of the cell
scaffolds.explain: A RIBOSOME is the cellular structure that MAKES PROTEINS by reading mRNA and assembling amino acids.

wrongExplains.0: Mitochondria produce energy; ribosomes make proteins.
wrongExplains.2: The nucleus is the control center; ribosomes make proteins.
wrongExplains.3: Transport proteins move substances; ribosomes synthesize proteins.

scaffolds.q: What is the difference between prokaryotic and eukaryotic cells?
options: Prokaryotes lack a membrane-bound nucleus; eukaryotes have one
scaffolds.explain: PROKARYOTES lack a membrane-bound NUCLEUS; EUKARYOTES have one. Bacteria are prokaryotes; human cells are eukaryotes.

wrongExplains.2: Prokaryotes are typically smaller than eukaryotes.

scaffolds.q: Are bacterial ribosomes identical to human ribosomes?
options: No, they differ in size and structure
scaffolds.explain: Bacterial ribosomes DIFFER IN SIZE AND STRUCTURE from human ribosomes. Bacterial = 70S; human = 80S (different subunit composition).

wrongExplains.0: They differ significantly in size and structure.
wrongExplains.2: All cells have ribosomes, including bacteria.
wrongExplains.3: They differ in both structure and some functional details.

scaffolds.q: What is the size of bacterial ribosomes?
scaffolds.explain: Bacterial ribosomes are 70S (made of 30S and 50S subunits). Human ribosomes are 80S. 'S' stands for Svedberg units.

wrongExplains.3: 50S is a subunit of the 70S ribosome, not the whole thing.

scaffolds.q: What is the size of human ribosomes?
scaffolds.explain: Human (eukaryotic) ribosomes are 80S (made of 40S and 60S subunits). Bacterial ribosomes are smaller at 70S.

wrongExplains.2: 60S is a subunit of 80S, not the whole ribosome.
wrongExplains.3: 40S is a subunit of 80S, not the whole ribosome.

scaffolds.q: Why do antibiotics target bacterial ribosomes?
options: To stop bacteria from making proteins, killing them
scaffolds.explain: Antibiotics target bacterial ribosomes to STOP BACTERIA FROM MAKING PROTEINS, which kills them or stops their growth.

wrongExplains.0: All living cells need ribosomes to make proteins.
wrongExplains.2: Antibiotics kill bacteria; they don't help them.
wrongExplains.3: Ribosomes are essential for life — protein synthesis is critical.

scaffolds.q: Why don't ribosome-targeting antibiotics harm human cells?
options: Human ribosomes have different structure, so the antibiotic doesn't bind
scaffolds.explain: Human ribosomes have DIFFERENT STRUCTURE (80S vs 70S), so antibiotics designed for 70S ribosomes DON'T BIND to human 80S ribosomes.

wrongExplains.2: Antibiotics work wherever bacteria are, not just hospitals.
wrongExplains.3: Human cells have ribosomes; they're just different from bacterial ones.

scaffolds.q: What does selective toxicity mean in antibiotic design?
options: The antibiotic targets bacterial structures while sparing human cells
scaffolds.explain: SELECTIVE TOXICITY means the antibiotic TARGETS BACTERIAL STRUCTURES while SPARING HUMAN CELLS. Ribosomal differences enable this.

wrongExplains.0: Selective means targeting specific cells, not all cells.
wrongExplains.2: Selective antibiotics target specific structures, not randomly.
wrongExplains.3: Selectivity refers to target specificity, not effectiveness.

scaffolds.q: Why is selectivity possible when targeting bacterial vs human ribosomes?
options: Bacterial and human ribosomes differ structurally (70S vs 80S)
Human cells are protected by skin
scaffolds.explain: Selectivity is possible because bacterial (70S) and human (80S) ribosomes DIFFER STRUCTURALLY. Drugs can bind one but not the other.

wrongExplains.0: Location doesn't determine selectivity; structure does.
wrongExplains.3: Skin doesn't explain why antibiotics don't affect internal human cells.
